title: Honors Biology — Semester 2 Final Practice
subject: Honors Biology
source: APIS Honors Biology Semester 2 Study Guide (2025–26)

questions.question: Which sequence correctly describes the Central Dogma of molecular biology?

choices.A: DNA → Protein → mRNA → Ribosome → Amino Acid
choices.B: DNA → mRNA → Ribosome → Amino Acid Chain → Protein

questions.explanation: The Central Dogma flows from DNA → mRNA (transcription) → Ribosome reads mRNA → Amino Acid Chain assembled → folds into a functional Protein.

questions.question: Why does the cell create an mRNA copy of DNA instead of using DNA directly during translation?

choices.A: DNA is too large to leave the nucleus
choices.B: DNA is too fragile to use in protein synthesis
choices.C: Ribosomes cannot read DNA's double helix
choices.D: DNA contains errors that mRNA fixes

questions.explanation: DNA stays in the nucleus because the chromosome is too large to pass through nuclear pores. mRNA is a portable, single-stranded copy that can travel to ribosomes in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: Which enzyme unzips DNA and adds complementary RNA nucleotides during transcription?
questions.explanation: RNA polymerase performs both functions: it unwinds the DNA template and pairs free RNA nucleotides (A, U, C, G) to the exposed bases.

questions.question: When DNA is transcribed into RNA, which base replaces thymine (T)?
questions.explanation: RNA uses uracil (U) instead of thymine (T). So in mRNA, A pairs with U (instead of T) when reading the DNA template.

questions.question: Where in the eukaryotic cell does transcription occur?
questions.explanation: Transcription occurs in the nucleus, where the DNA is housed. The mRNA is then exported to the cytoplasm for translation.

questions.question: What is a codon?

choices.B: A group of three mRNA nucleotides that codes for one amino acid

questions.explanation: A codon is a triplet of mRNA bases (e.g., AUG, UUC) that the ribosome reads to specify a single amino acid in the growing polypeptide.

questions.question: What is the role of tRNA molecules during translation?

choices.B: They carry matching amino acids to the ribosome using their anticodons

questions.explanation: Each tRNA has an anticodon that base-pairs with an mRNA codon, delivering the corresponding amino acid so the ribosome can add it to the growing chain.

questions.question: Where does translation take place in the cell?

choices.B: Ribosomes (in the cytoplasm or on the rough ER)

questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the site of translation. They can be free in the cytoplasm or attached to the rough endoplasmic reticulum.

questions.question: Why are proteins called the "physical expression of genes"?

choices.A: Because they store genetic information for the next generation
choices.B: Because they make up the cell membrane only
choices.C: Because they determine traits and carry out cellular functions

questions.explanation: Proteins build structures (e.g., hair, enzymes, receptors) and carry out the cell's functions, so a gene's effect is realized when its protein is made.

questions.question: What best defines a mutation?

choices.A: A temporary change in protein folding
choices.B: A permanent change to the base sequence of DNA
choices.C: A reversible chemical modification of an amino acid

questions.explanation: A mutation is a permanent alteration to the DNA base sequence. It can be caused by replication errors or by external mutagens.

questions.question: A substitution mutation occurs when:

choices.A: One DNA base is replaced by another
choices.B: A base is added to the sequence
choices.C: A base is removed from the sequence

questions.explanation: Substitution swaps one base for another (e.g., A→G). Insertions and deletions cause frameshift mutations instead.

questions.question: Why do some substitution mutations have no effect on the protein produced?

choices.B: Because of the redundancy of the genetic code — multiple codons can specify the same amino acid
choices.C: Because ribosomes skip the mutated codon
choices.D: Because tRNA always finds the correct amino acid

questions.explanation: Several different codons can code for the same amino acid (e.g., UUU and UUC both code for phenylalanine), so a substitution may not change the amino acid. These are called silent mutations.

questions.question: Why do frameshift mutations (insertions or deletions) usually destroy protein function?

choices.B: They shift the reading frame, changing every codon downstream

questions.explanation: Adding or deleting bases (not in multiples of three) shifts how codons are read. Every triplet after the mutation is altered, usually producing a non-functional protein.

questions.question: Which type of mutation can be passed to offspring?
questions.explanation: Only mutations in gametes (germ-line cells) are passed to the next generation. Somatic mutations affect the individual but are not inherited.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a mutagen?
questions.explanation: Mutagens are external agents that increase mutation rates. UV radiation, X-rays, and certain chemicals (e.g., benzene) are common mutagens.

questions.question: What is the expected phenotypic ratio of a dihybrid cross between two heterozygotes (e.g., AaBb × AaBb), with two unlinked traits showing simple dominance?
questions.explanation: AaBb × AaBb produces a 9:3:3:1 ratio: 9 with both dominant traits, 3 with one dominant and one recessive, 3 with the opposite combo, and 1 with both recessive.

questions.question: Using FOIL, which gametes does a parent with genotype AaBb produce?
questions.explanation: FOIL (First, Outer, Inner, Last) on AaBb gives the four allele combinations: AB, Ab, aB, ab — the four possible gametes (assuming unlinked genes).

questions.question: How do linked genes affect the standard dihybrid 9:3:3:1 ratio?

choices.A: They have no effect on the ratio
choices.B: They produce only the 9:3:3:1 ratio more reliably
choices.C: They alter the ratio because the genes are inherited together more often than expected
choices.D: They convert the ratio to a 3:1

questions.explanation: Genes located close together on the same chromosome tend to be inherited as a unit, so parental allele combinations show up more often than predicted by 9:3:3:1.

questions.question: What phenotypic ratio is typically observed when an allele is lethal in the homozygous state and parents are both heterozygous?
questions.explanation: Of the expected 1:2:1 genotypic ratio, the homozygous-lethal class dies (often in the embryo). The surviving ratio of heterozygotes to the other homozygote is 2:1.

questions.question: Which inheritance pattern is illustrated by AB blood type, where both A and B antigens appear on red blood cells?
questions.explanation: Codominance means both alleles are fully and equally expressed in the heterozygote. AB blood type expresses both A and B antigens — neither is masked.

questions.question: Pink flowers from a cross of red and white parents is a classic example of which inheritance pattern?
questions.explanation: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygous phenotype is a blend of the two homozygous phenotypes — so red × white yields pink, not red or both colors.

questions.question: On a pedigree chart, what does a square represent?
questions.explanation: Squares represent males. Circles represent females. A shaded shape (of either type) indicates an individual affected by the trait being tracked.

questions.question: On a pedigree chart, a shaded shape (square or circle) represents:

choices.B: An individual affected by the trait

questions.explanation: Shading indicates that the individual expresses (is affected by) the trait being tracked through the family.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes codominance from incomplete dominance?

choices.A: Codominance produces a blended phenotype; incomplete dominance shows both phenotypes side by side
choices.B: Codominance shows both alleles fully expressed; incomplete dominance produces a blended phenotype

questions.explanation: Codominance: both alleles equally and fully expressed (e.g., AB blood). Incomplete dominance: heterozygote shows a blend of the two homozygous phenotypes (e.g., pink flowers).

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a major source of genetic variation in a population?

choices.B: Crossing over and independent assortment in meiosis

questions.explanation: Mitosis produces genetically identical daughter cells, so it does NOT contribute to inherited genetic variation. Meiosis, mutations, and gene flow do.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of continuous variation?
questions.explanation: Continuous variation shows a range of values along a spectrum (height, hand span). Discontinuous variation falls into distinct categories (blood type, tongue rolling).

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of discontinuous variation?
questions.explanation: Discontinuous variation has distinct categories with no in-between values. Blood type is either A, B, AB, or O — there is no "half-A" type.

questions.question: Which of the following is a STRUCTURAL adaptation?
questions.explanation: Structural adaptations are physical body features (fur, wings, beak shape). Migration is behavioral; venom production is physiological; nocturnal activity is behavioral.

questions.question: Producing venom for defense or hunting is best classified as which type of adaptation?
questions.explanation: Physiological adaptations involve internal biochemical processes — venom production, temperature regulation, hibernation chemistry. They are not visible body parts (structural) or actions (behavioral).

questions.question: Natural selection acts on individuals based on:

choices.A: Their ability to find a mate
choices.B: Their fit to the environment, which determines survival and reproduction

questions.explanation: Natural selection favors traits that improve survival and reproduction in a given environment. Better-adapted individuals leave more offspring, shifting allele frequencies over time.

questions.question: A peacock's elaborate tail is an example of which selection pressure?
questions.explanation: Sexual selection favors traits that improve mating success, even at a cost to survival. Peacock tails attract peahens but make the male more visible to predators.

questions.question: Which scenario best illustrates the FOUNDER effect (a form of genetic drift)?

choices.A: A natural disaster wipes out 90% of a large population, leaving a small group with skewed allele frequencies
choices.B: A small group of individuals leaves to colonize a new island, carrying only a subset of the original gene pool
choices.C: A predator selectively eats only camouflaged prey

questions.explanation: Founder effect: a small founding group establishes a new population, so its allele frequencies differ from the original population by chance. Bottleneck (option A) is the disaster scenario.

questions.question: Which mRNA codon signals the START of translation in most organisms?
questions.explanation: AUG codes for methionine and serves as the START codon, telling the ribosome where to begin translating. UAA, UGA, and UAG are all STOP codons.

questions.question: How many STOP codons are there in the standard genetic code?
questions.explanation: There are three STOP codons: UAA, UAG, and UGA. They do not code for any amino acid; instead they release the completed polypeptide from the ribosome.

questions.question: What mRNA sequence is produced from the DNA template strand 3'-TACGGA-5'?
questions.explanation: Read 3'→5' on the template, build mRNA 5'→3': T→A, A→U, C→G, G→C, G→C, A→U. So 3'-TACGGA-5' → 5'-AUGCCU-3'. Note U replaces T in RNA.

questions.question: If a codon is 3 nucleotides long and there are 4 possible bases (A, U, C, G), how many distinct codons are possible?
questions.explanation: $4^3 = 64$ possible codons. Of these, 61 code for amino acids and 3 are STOP codons. There are only 20 standard amino acids, so the code is redundant.

questions.question: If an mRNA codon is 5'-GCA-3', what is the corresponding tRNA anticodon?
questions.explanation: The anticodon is antiparallel and complementary to the codon. mRNA 5'-GCA-3' pairs with tRNA 3'-CGU-5' (reading anticodon in 3'→5' direction).

questions.question: What is the difference between a polypeptide and a protein?

choices.B: A polypeptide is a chain of amino acids; a protein is a folded, functional polypeptide (or set of polypeptides)
choices.C: A polypeptide is made of nucleotides; a protein is made of amino acids
choices.D: A polypeptide is shorter than 100 amino acids; a protein is longer

questions.explanation: Translation produces a polypeptide — a linear chain of amino acids. Once the chain folds into its 3D shape (and possibly combines with other chains), it becomes a functional protein.

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about RNA but NOT about DNA?

choices.B: Contains the sugar ribose and the base uracil

questions.explanation: RNA uses ribose (DNA uses deoxyribose) and uracil (DNA uses thymine). RNA is also typically single-stranded, while DNA is double-stranded.

questions.question: Place these steps of protein synthesis in the correct order: (1) Ribosome reads mRNA codons, (2) RNA polymerase makes mRNA from DNA, (3) tRNA delivers amino acids, (4) Polypeptide folds into protein.
questions.explanation: Transcription first (2: DNA → mRNA), then translation: ribosome reads codons (1) while tRNAs deliver matching amino acids (3), and finally the chain folds (4).

questions.question: How many standard amino acids are used to build proteins in living organisms?
questions.explanation: There are 20 standard amino acids. The genetic code has 64 codons, but multiple codons can code for the same amino acid (redundancy).

questions.question: Which statement best defines a gene?

choices.A: A protein that determines a trait
choices.B: A segment of DNA that codes for a functional product (typically a protein)

questions.explanation: A gene is a stretch of DNA that contains the instructions for making a protein (or a functional RNA). Many genes together make up a chromosome.

questions.question: Which template does RNA polymerase use to build an mRNA molecule?
questions.explanation: RNA polymerase reads the antisense (template) strand of DNA in the 3'→5' direction and synthesizes a complementary mRNA in the 5'→3' direction. The mRNA sequence matches the sense strand (with U for T).

questions.question: Which type of substitution mutation changes one amino acid into a different amino acid?
questions.explanation: Missense: substitution changes the codon to one that codes for a different amino acid. Silent: same amino acid. Nonsense: changes to a STOP codon. Frameshift: insertion/deletion.

questions.question: What is a nonsense mutation?

choices.A: A mutation that has no effect on the protein
choices.B: A substitution that changes a codon into a STOP codon, prematurely ending translation
choices.C: A mutation that adds extra DNA bases
choices.D: A mutation that occurs only in non-coding DNA

questions.explanation: Nonsense mutations turn an amino-acid codon into a STOP codon, truncating the protein. The resulting shortened protein is usually non-functional.

questions.question: Why is an INSERTION of one base usually more harmful to a protein than a SUBSTITUTION of one base?

choices.A: Insertions are larger and slower to repair
choices.B: Insertions shift the reading frame, altering every codon downstream; substitutions affect only one codon
choices.D: Insertions occur only in essential genes

questions.explanation: A single-base insertion shifts the entire reading frame, changing every codon after the insertion point. A substitution changes only the one codon containing the changed base.

questions.question: Which mutation is LEAST likely to affect the protein produced by a gene?

choices.A: A frameshift in the middle of an exon
choices.B: A nonsense mutation early in the coding region
choices.C: A substitution deep within an intron (non-coding region)
choices.D: A deletion of the start codon

questions.explanation: Introns are removed during mRNA processing, so most mutations within introns do not affect the final protein. The other options all directly damage the protein.

questions.question: Cancer is often caused by accumulated mutations in which type of cells?
questions.explanation: Cancer arises from somatic (body) cell mutations that disrupt cell-cycle control, leading to uncontrolled growth. These mutations are not inherited but cause disease in the individual.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a CHEMICAL mutagen?
questions.explanation: Chemical mutagens include cigarette smoke compounds, asbestos, and certain pesticides. UV, X-rays, and cosmic rays are physical (radiation) mutagens.

questions.topic: Sickle cell as a missense example
questions.question: Sickle-cell anemia is caused by a single base substitution that changes one amino acid in hemoglobin. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Sickle-cell is the textbook example of a missense mutation: GAG → GTG changes glutamic acid to valine in the beta-globin chain, deforming red blood cells.

questions.question: Why do most random mutations not cause obvious problems in an organism?

choices.A: Cells have DNA repair enzymes that fix many mutations
choices.B: Most mutations are silent, are in non-coding DNA, or are repaired before they matter
choices.D: Mutations only happen in non-essential genes

questions.explanation: Both factors apply. DNA repair enzymes (e.g., proofreading by polymerase) fix many errors, and many mutations land in introns, intergenic regions, or are silent.

questions.question: Which statement about mutations is MOST accurate?

choices.C: Most mutations are neutral; a few are harmful, and rarely some are beneficial

questions.explanation: Most mutations are neutral (silent or in non-coding regions). A small fraction is harmful, and an even smaller fraction is beneficial — these rare beneficial mutations fuel evolution.

questions.question: Which insertion or deletion would NOT cause a frameshift mutation?
questions.explanation: Inserting or deleting bases in multiples of 3 keeps the reading frame intact (just adds or removes one whole amino acid). Other amounts shift the frame.

questions.question: Which environmental factor would MOST increase a population's mutation rate?
questions.explanation: UV radiation damages DNA directly (e.g., forming thymine dimers), increasing mutation rates. The other factors do not directly cause genetic change.

questions.question: A man develops a skin cancer mutation in middle age. Will his children inherit this mutation?

choices.A: Yes, all body cell mutations are inherited
choices.B: No, mutations in skin cells are somatic and not passed on
choices.D: Only if the father has more than one child

questions.explanation: Skin cells are somatic. Only mutations in germ-line cells (sperm or egg) are passed to offspring. Acquired somatic mutations affect only the individual.

questions.question: A monohybrid cross between two heterozygous parents (Aa × Aa) for a simple dominant trait gives what phenotypic ratio?
questions.explanation: Aa × Aa: offspring are 1 AA, 2 Aa, 1 aa. With simple dominance, AA and Aa share the dominant phenotype, giving 3 dominant : 1 recessive.

questions.question: For Aa × Aa with incomplete dominance, the phenotypic ratio is:
questions.explanation: With incomplete dominance, the heterozygote (Aa) has a distinct phenotype (a blend), so the phenotypic ratio matches the genotypic ratio: 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a test cross?

choices.B: To determine whether an individual showing a dominant trait is homozygous (AA) or heterozygous (Aa)

questions.explanation: A test cross pairs the unknown individual with a homozygous recessive (aa). If any recessive offspring appear, the unknown must be heterozygous (Aa).

questions.question: If two heterozygous (Aa) parents produce a child, what is the probability the child is homozygous recessive (aa)?
questions.explanation: Aa × Aa Punnett square: AA, Aa, Aa, aa. The aa box is 1 of 4 = 1/4 probability.

questions.question: A trait carried on the X chromosome (e.g., color blindness, recessive) shows up more often in which sex, and why?

choices.A: Females, because they have two X chromosomes
choices.B: Males, because they have only one X chromosome (no second X to mask the recessive allele)
choices.D: Only in males, never in females

questions.explanation: Males (XY) have only one X chromosome, so a single recessive X-linked allele is expressed. Females (XX) need two copies to express the trait, making it rarer in them.

questions.question: Human ABO blood type is controlled by three alleles: I^A, I^B, and i. A person with genotype I^A i has what blood type?
questions.explanation: I^A and I^B are codominant with each other, but both are dominant over i. So I^A i expresses only the A antigen — type A blood.

questions.question: Human skin color and height show a continuous range of phenotypes. This is best explained by:

choices.A: A single gene with two alleles

questions.explanation: Polygenic traits are controlled by many genes, each adding a small effect. The combined contributions create a continuous distribution rather than discrete categories.

questions.question: On a pedigree, two unaffected parents have an affected child. The trait is most likely:
questions.explanation: If neither parent shows the trait but a child does, both parents must be heterozygous carriers of a recessive allele (Aa × Aa → 1/4 aa).

questions.question: On a pedigree chart, what does a horizontal line connecting a square and a circle represent?
questions.explanation: A horizontal line between a square (male) and a circle (female) indicates a mating pair. Vertical lines descending from this line lead to their offspring.

questions.question: In a 9:3:3:1 dihybrid ratio of 320 offspring, approximately how many display BOTH dominant traits?
questions.explanation: 9 of 16 = 9/16 × 320 = 180 offspring with both dominant traits.

questions.question: If one parent is heterozygous (Aa) for an autosomal dominant disease and the other is homozygous unaffected (aa), what is the chance each child inherits the disease?
questions.explanation: Aa × aa: half of offspring are Aa (affected), half are aa (unaffected). 50%.

questions.question: What does it mean for a person to be a 'carrier' of a recessive genetic disorder?

choices.A: They have two copies of the recessive allele and show the disease
choices.B: They are heterozygous (Aa): they do not show the disease but can pass the recessive allele to children
choices.C: They have caught the disease from another person
choices.D: They are immune to the disease

questions.explanation: Carriers are heterozygotes who carry one copy of the recessive disease allele but appear unaffected because the dominant allele masks it. They can pass the allele to offspring.

questions.question: A cross between a homozygous red flower (RR) and a homozygous white flower (WW) showing CODOMINANCE produces what F1 phenotype?

choices.D: Red and white spotted (both colors visible)

questions.explanation: In codominance both alleles are fully expressed, so the heterozygote shows BOTH colors (e.g., red and white patches). Pink (a blend) would be incomplete dominance.

questions.question: Crossing over during meiosis contributes to genetic variation by:

choices.B: Exchanging segments between homologous chromosomes, creating new allele combinations

questions.explanation: Crossing over swaps DNA segments between homologous chromosomes during prophase I of meiosis, producing recombinant chromosomes with new mixes of parental alleles.

questions.question: Independent assortment in meiosis means:

choices.A: All chromosomes always go to the same daughter cell
choices.B: Each pair of homologous chromosomes separates independently of the others, creating new combinations

questions.explanation: During metaphase I, each homologous pair lines up independently, so the maternal/paternal mix in each gamete is random. With 23 human chromosome pairs, this gives $2^{23}$ possible combinations.

questions.question: Gene flow refers to:

choices.A: Mutation rate within a single individual
choices.B: The movement of alleles between populations through migration and interbreeding
choices.D: The spread of mRNA in a cell

questions.explanation: Gene flow happens when individuals (or pollen, gametes) move between populations and reproduce, transferring alleles and reducing genetic differences between populations.

questions.question: Which is an example of a PHYSIOLOGICAL adaptation?

choices.B: Hibernation chemistry that lets a bear survive winter
choices.C: A wolf hunting in a pack

questions.explanation: Physiological adaptations are internal biochemical or metabolic processes (hibernation chemistry, venom synthesis, salt regulation). Wings/shell = structural; pack hunting = behavioral.

questions.question: A skunk spraying a foul liquid to deter predators is an example of which type of adaptation?
questions.explanation: Spraying is a learned/instinctive ACTION, so it is behavioral. The chemical itself is produced physiologically, but the ACT of spraying is behavior.

questions.question: A volcanic eruption nearly wipes out a population, leaving only 12 survivors with allele frequencies very different from the original group. This is:
questions.explanation: Bottleneck: a sudden drastic reduction in population size leaves a small group whose allele frequencies differ from the original by chance. Founder = small group COLONIZES new area.

questions.question: Why is genetic variation important for the long-term survival of a species?

choices.B: It provides the raw material for natural selection — some individuals may have traits that allow them to survive when conditions change

questions.explanation: If all individuals are genetically identical, an environmental change (disease, climate shift) could wipe out the entire species. Variation means at least some individuals may survive and pass on adaptive alleles.

questions.question: Which trait shows DISCONTINUOUS variation?
questions.explanation: Discontinuous variation has distinct categories with no in-between values. Tongue rolling is yes/no. Hand span, foot length, and weight are all continuous (measurable on a spectrum).

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical selection pressure on a population?
questions.explanation: Selection pressures are environmental challenges (predators, disease, competition, climate). Genetic drift is a separate evolutionary force based on chance, not on environmental fitness.

questions.question: In evolutionary biology, an organism's 'fitness' is best measured by:

choices.C: How many viable offspring it produces that survive to reproduce

questions.explanation: Fitness = reproductive success. An organism that lives long but has no offspring contributes nothing to the next generation's gene pool. Fitness counts surviving descendants.

questions.question: Genetic drift has a stronger effect on:
questions.explanation: In small populations, random sampling effects (which individuals reproduce) can dramatically shift allele frequencies. In large populations, random fluctuations average out.

questions.question: Which statement correctly summarizes the relationship between variation and evolution?

choices.B: Variation is the raw material on which natural selection acts; without variation, evolution cannot occur
choices.D: Variation only matters in plants, not animals

questions.explanation: Variation must exist first (from mutations, meiosis, gene flow). Then natural selection or drift can change which alleles become more common. No variation = no evolution.
